description: Triggering accuracy and domain correctness tests for finance skills

evals.prompt: My client wants to roll their 401k into an IRA with us. We're a dual-registrant — I handle some accounts as a broker and some as an adviser. Which standard of care applies to this rollover recommendation, and what documentation do I need to keep? The account is about $340K and they're 58 years old.
evals.expected_output: Should identify this as a Reg BI recommendation (rollover from ERISA plan to IRA via broker-dealer capacity), cite the four Reg BI obligations (Disclosure, Care, Conflict of Interest, Compliance), mention PTE 2020-02 for the ERISA side, and discuss documentation of reasonably available alternatives. Should NOT default to fiduciary analysis unless the adviser capacity is explicitly chosen.

assertions.text: Mentions Reg BI by name
Cites the four Reg BI obligations (Disclosure, Care, Conflict of Interest, Compliance)
Mentions PTE 2020-02 or DOL prohibited transaction exemption
Discusses reasonably available alternatives analysis
Does not incorrectly apply pure fiduciary standard without acknowledging BD capacity

evals.prompt: We're an RIA and one of our advisers just recommended a proprietary fund to a client when a cheaper third-party option with similar performance exists. The client is a retired teacher, moderate risk tolerance, $600K portfolio. Is there a fiduciary issue here?
evals.expected_output: Should apply IA Act Section 206 fiduciary analysis — duty of care (was the recommendation in the client's best interest given cheaper alternative?) and duty of loyalty (proprietary fund creates a conflict). Should discuss whether the conflict was disclosed, mitigated, and whether the adviser can demonstrate the recommendation was still in the client's best interest despite the cost differential.

assertions.text: Applies IA Act Section 206 fiduciary framework
Analyzes both duty of care and duty of loyalty
Identifies proprietary fund recommendation as a conflict of interest
Discusses whether cost differential is material and justified
Does not incorrectly apply FINRA suitability framework as the primary standard

evals.prompt: I'm building out the fee schedule section of our Form ADV Part 2A. We charge a tiered AUM fee (1.0% on first $1M, 0.75% on next $2M, 0.50% above $3M), plus a $2,500 annual financial planning fee. We also get 12b-1 fees from some mutual fund positions. How should we disclose all of this?
evals.expected_output: Should focus on disclosure requirements — Form ADV Item 5 structure, how to present the tiered AUM schedule, requirement to disclose the financial planning fee as a separate service, obligation to disclose 12b-1 fees as indirect compensation creating a conflict, and reference to Reg BI CRS fee disclosure if applicable. Should NOT focus on billing mechanics (how to calculate, prorate, or collect fees).

assertions.text: References Form ADV Part 2A Item 5
Addresses 12b-1 fee disclosure as indirect compensation or conflict
Discusses how to present tiered fee schedules in disclosure documents
Focuses on regulatory disclosure requirements, not billing system implementation

evals.prompt: I need to set up our quarterly billing process. We bill in advance on AUM as of the last business day of the prior quarter, with new accounts prorated from their funding date. When a client terminates mid-quarter we refund the unused portion. How should I structure the billing calculation and what edge cases do I need to handle?
evals.expected_output: Should focus on billing mechanics — AUM valuation date selection, proration formulas for new/terminated accounts, mid-quarter termination refund calculation, handling of account additions/withdrawals during the quarter, billing frequency, custodian fee deduction setup, and reconciliation. Should NOT primarily discuss disclosure obligations.

assertions.text: Provides proration formula or methodology for partial-quarter accounts
Addresses mid-quarter termination refund calculation
Discusses billing system mechanics (custodian deduction, valuation dates)
Focuses on operational billing implementation, not regulatory disclosure

evals.prompt: We're onboarding a new institutional client — a family trust with three trustees, two of whom are US persons and one is a UK resident. The trust document names a corporate trustee as well. Walk me through the compliance requirements for opening this account.
evals.expected_output: Should focus on CIP/KYC verification for all trustees (including the foreign person), beneficial ownership certification for the entity, OFAC screening of all parties, risk-based approval tier (elevated due to foreign trustee), enhanced due diligence triggers, and documentation requirements. Should NOT focus on operational workflow steps like NIGO management or custodian submission mechanics.

assertions.text: Addresses CIP/KYC for each trustee individually
Mentions beneficial ownership certification requirement
Flags the non-US trustee as requiring enhanced due diligence
References OFAC or sanctions screening

evals.prompt: Our NIGO rate for new accounts submitted to Schwab is running at 28%. Most rejections are missing signatures, incorrect entity titling, and incomplete beneficiary designations. I need to redesign our account opening workflow to get this under 10%. What should the new process look like?
evals.expected_output: Should focus on operational workflow redesign — document validation gates, pre-submission QC checks, digital signature integration, common titling error patterns and how to prevent them, NIGO root cause analysis, STP rate improvement tactics, and custodian submission best practices.

assertions.text: Addresses NIGO root cause analysis and specific rejection patterns
Proposes workflow validation gates or pre-submission checks
Discusses operational process improvement (not just compliance requirements)
Mentions specific custodian integration or submission mechanics

evals.prompt: I'm looking at a fund that returned 12% annualized over the last 5 years with a standard deviation of 18%. The max drawdown was -32% during COVID. How do I assess whether the risk-adjusted returns are actually good, and what metrics should I use to compare it against its benchmark?
evals.expected_output: Should analyze realized/historical risk metrics — Sharpe ratio calculation from the given data, Sortino ratio, maximum drawdown analysis, recovery time, comparison to benchmark using tracking error and information ratio. Should ground analysis in the historical data provided, not project future scenarios.

assertions.text: Calculates or discusses Sharpe ratio using provided return and volatility data
Analyzes the -32% max drawdown in context (severity, recovery)
Mentions Sortino ratio or downside risk metrics
Analysis is grounded in historical/realized data, not forward projections

evals.prompt: I'm building a new portfolio for a client with a 15-year horizon. I need to estimate the probability that a 70/30 stock/bond allocation will fail to meet their 6% real return target. What risk models and simulation approaches should I use?
evals.expected_output: Should discuss forward-looking risk modeling — Monte Carlo simulation, capital market assumptions (expected returns, volatilities, correlations), scenario analysis, regime-switching models, fat-tail adjustments. Should focus on projecting future outcomes, not analyzing past performance.

assertions.text: Discusses Monte Carlo simulation or stochastic modeling
Mentions capital market assumptions or expected return estimation
Addresses probability of meeting the return target over the time horizon
Focus is on forward projection methodology, not historical analysis

evals.prompt: Our account transfer process currently requires manual intervention for about 60% of incoming ACATs. The ops team is copying data between three systems and manually matching transfer items against our records. I want to automate this to get our STP rate above 90%. What approach should I take?
evals.expected_output: Should focus on straight-through processing methodology — measuring current STP rate, identifying manual touchpoints, exception-based processing design, API vs RPA evaluation for the three-system integration, exception categorization and auto-resolution rules, and STP benchmarking targets for transfer processing.

assertions.text: Defines or calculates STP rate and sets improvement targets
Identifies exception categories and auto-resolution patterns
Discusses API vs RPA integration approaches for legacy systems
Focus is on end-to-end automation and exception handling, not task routing or approvals

evals.prompt: I need to build an approval workflow for account maintenance requests. Address changes need one approval, beneficiary changes need two approvals with a compliance review, and re-registrations need three approvals including a supervisor sign-off. Each level has different SLA targets and escalation rules. How should I design this?
evals.expected_output: Should focus on workflow design — multi-level approval chains, delegation of authority, SLA monitoring and escalation rules, task routing logic, state machine modeling for the different request types, and audit trail requirements.

assertions.text: Designs multi-level approval chain with different thresholds by request type
Addresses SLA targets and escalation rules for aging items
Discusses task routing or state machine modeling
Focus is on approval chains and routing, not end-to-end STP rate optimization

evals.prompt: A client invested $100,000 in a fund. After 6 months the fund was up 15% and they added another $50,000. After another 6 months the total value is $178,000. Calculate both the time-weighted return (TWR) and the money-weighted return (MWR/IRR) and explain why they differ.
evals.expected_output: TWR: Sub-period 1 return = 15%. Sub-period 2: start value = $115K + $50K = $165K, end = $178K, return = 7.88%. TWR = (1.15)(1.0788) - 1 = 24.06%. MWR/IRR: solve -100K at t=0, -50K at t=0.5, +178K at t=1. IRR will be lower than TWR because more capital was deployed after the stronger period. Should clearly explain the divergence.

assertions.text: TWR calculation is approximately 24% (range 23.5-24.5%)
MWR/IRR is lower than TWR and explains why (more capital after strong period)
Shows sub-period return calculations step by step
Correctly identifies TWR as manager skill measure and MWR as investor experience

evals.prompt: We're a broker-dealer and one of our reps has a turnover ratio of 8.2 and a cost-to-equity ratio of 14% in a retired client's account. The client is 72 years old with moderate risk tolerance and a $420K account. The rep says the client approved every trade. What are the compliance issues?
evals.expected_output: Should identify quantitative suitability (FINRA Rule 2111 third prong) violations — turnover ratio above 6 is presumptively excessive, cost-to-equity ratio of 14% means the account must earn 14% just to break even. Client approval does not excuse excessive trading. Should flag churning indicators, senior investor protections, and recommended supervisory response.

assertions.text: Cites FINRA Rule 2111 quantitative suitability prong
Identifies turnover ratio above 6 as presumptively excessive
Explains cost-to-equity ratio meaning (account must earn 14% to break even)
States that client approval of individual trades does not cure excessive trading
Mentions senior investor protections given client age

evals.prompt: I have a taxable portfolio worth $800K that has drifted from its 60/40 target to 68/32 stocks/bonds. My threshold band is +/- 5%. I also have about $15K in unrealized losses in some equity positions. Should I rebalance, and if so, how should I coordinate with tax-loss harvesting?
evals.expected_output: Should identify that equities have breached the 5% band (68% vs 65% upper limit). Recommend selling ~$64K in equities to buy bonds. Should coordinate with TLH — harvest the $15K in losses first by selling losing positions as part of the rebalance sell, replace with similar (not substantially identical) securities to maintain exposure, and be aware of wash-sale rules (30-day window, across all accounts including retirement). Net effect: rebalance AND capture tax alpha.

assertions.text: Identifies that the 5% band has been breached (68% > 65% upper limit)
Calculates approximate trade size needed to rebalance (~$64K)
Recommends harvesting the $15K loss as part of the rebalance
Warns about wash-sale rules (30-day window, substantially identical securities)
Mentions replacement security selection to maintain market exposure

evals.prompt: I'm preparing for a quarterly review meeting with a retired couple (ages 68 and 65). They have a $1.8M portfolio across IRA, Roth IRA, and taxable accounts. The husband turns 73 next year and will need to start RMDs. What should my review prep cover?
evals.expected_output: Should cover: performance vs benchmark and IPS targets, asset allocation drift check, RMD estimation for the husband's IRA (using Uniform Lifetime Table), tax-efficient withdrawal sequencing across the three account types, Roth conversion opportunity analysis before RMDs begin, income projection for the coming year, insurance review, and estate planning coordination.

assertions.text: Addresses RMD calculation and timing (husband turning 73)
Discusses tax-efficient withdrawal sequencing across IRA/Roth/taxable
Mentions Roth conversion opportunity before RMDs begin
Includes performance review against IPS or benchmark

evals.prompt: Walk me through what happens to a market order to buy 5,000 shares of AAPL from the moment I hit submit to when the shares appear in my account. Cover routing, execution, clearing, and settlement.
evals.expected_output: Should cover the full lifecycle: order entry and validation, smart order routing (consideration of exchanges, dark pools, Reg NMS Rule 611 order protection), execution venue selection, trade execution and fill confirmation, post-trade allocation, NSCC trade comparison and netting (CNS system), DTC settlement on T+1, and final position update in the client's account.

assertions.text: Mentions smart order routing and venue selection considerations
References Reg NMS or order protection rule
Describes NSCC clearing and netting process
States T+1 settlement cycle for US equities

evals.prompt: A client wants to transfer their $2.1M account from another broker-dealer to us via ACAT. The account has stocks, bonds, mutual funds, and some options positions. The sending firm is being slow — it's been 5 business days and we haven't received the assets. What are our options?
evals.expected_output: Should explain ACAT process and FINRA Rule 11870 timeline (3 business days to validate, then deliver within timeline). After 5 days, the sending firm may be in violation. Options: file a FINRA complaint, escalate via NSCC reclaim process. Should note that options may need special handling (not all options transfer via ACAT), mutual fund positions may need to be liquidated if not on the receiving firm's platform, and the client should be advised about potential costs.

assertions.text: References FINRA Rule 11870 and the ACAT timeline requirements
Identifies recourse options (FINRA complaint, NSCC escalation)
Notes that some assets may not transfer cleanly (options, proprietary funds)
Mentions the 3-business-day validation requirement
